summary:
Lapis lazuli, the vivid blue semi-precious stone mined in the Badakhshan region of northeastern Afghanistan at deposits in the Kokcha River valley, was among the most prized luxury commodities of the ancient world, traded across vast distances from its single known ancient source to civilisations throughout the Near East, the Mediterranean, and the Indus Valley. Evidence of lapis lazuli trade dates to at least 2800–2000 BC, when the stone appears in elite burials and royal treasuries from Mesopotamia to Egypt and the [[event:46|Indus Valley]].

The [[event:1727|Royal Cemetery of Ur]] contained lapis lazuli in spectacular quantities — inlaid in the [[event:1787|Standard of Ur]], crafted into jewellery and headdresses, and deposited as objects of royal prestige — demonstrating that the Sumerian elite actively sought this stone from sources thousands of kilometres distant. Egyptian royal objects including the mask of Tutankhamun (a later period) and numerous Old Kingdom artefacts also feature lapis lazuli prominently. In Mesopotamian mythology, lapis lazuli was associated with the divine and with the heavens, reinforcing its status as a prestige material reserved for rulers and gods.

The lapis lazuli trade is one of the clearest demonstrations of the long-distance commercial networks that integrated the ancient world during the third millennium BC. The stone's consistent presence in elite contexts across Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Indus Valley — all thousands of kilometres from its single Afghan source — implies organised trade routes, trusted intermediaries, and the economic capacity of these civilisations to finance the import of luxury goods over vast distances. Lapis lazuli thus serves as a material tracer of the commercial connections underlying Bronze Age globalisation.
